name: Shelter Construction (Basic)
tagline: Windbreaks, lean-tos, and simple enclosed spaces that stabilise temperature and protect people, fire, and stores
problem:
Humans tolerate a narrow comfort band without clothing and fire support. Wind strips convective heat; rain destroys insulation; sun causes dehydration; predators exploit sleeping vulnerability. The basic shelter problem is creating a bounded microclimate: reducing wind speed, shedding precipitation, reflecting radiant heat from fire, and separating occupants from damp ground.

This is not aesthetics — it is thermodynamics and hygiene. Raised sleeping platforms reduce parasitic insect contact; overhangs keep fuel dry; smoke holes balance ventilation versus heat retention. In cold climates, small enclosed volumes around a fire are dramatically easier to heat than open camps.

Shelter also protects technology: cordage rots faster when constantly wet; clay dries unevenly in gusts; charcoal kilns need wind shields. A civilisation rebuilding needs the very first week to include reliable nightly shelter, not only because of comfort but because cognitive work (tool repair, planning) requires dry hands and stable light.
overview:
Basic shelters combine structural members, covering, and anchoring. Structural members are poles harvested green or dry, lashed into tripods, A-frames, or ridge-pole configurations. Coverings include thatch, bark sheets, hides, woven mats, and snow blocks in polar contexts. Anchoring uses stakes, rocks, and tensioned cordage in guy lines.

Site selection dominates performance: avoid drainage channels, look for natural windbreaks, position openings away from prevailing storms, and place fire where smoke vents without filling the sleeping volume. In forest, dead standing timber is often straighter than ground-fall that has begun to rot.

Lashing is the joining technology: square lashings resist shear, diagonal lashings grip crossing poles, shear lashings bundle parallel members. Friction wraps must be seated under tension — slip knots fail in wind gusts.

Thermal upgrades include reflective fire walls (stones, clay), partial burrows, and insulating layers of leaves or grass between inner and outer skins. These are low-tech but map directly to later principles of insulation, vapour barriers, and ventilation in permanent architecture.

principles.name: Wind pressure and permeability
principles.explanation: Wind loads scale with velocity squared; openings and loose thatch create pumping leaks. Sealing the lowest windward edge often matters more than perfect ridge sealing.

principles.name: Stack effect ventilation
principles.explanation: Warm smoke rises; a high vent draws air low — without a low inlet you get backdraft and smoke pooling at sleeping height.

principles.name: Ground conduction
principles.explanation: Earth is a large heat sink; bedding platforms interrupt conductive loss even if air temperature seems acceptable.

principles.name: Material creep under load
principles.explanation: Green wood shrinks as it dries; lashings loosen. Retension after first nights is mandatory maintenance, not failure.

components.name: Ridge pole or lintel
components.function: Spans opening, supports roof loads, defines shelter height
components.criticalNote: Check for heart rot — sound wood rings audibly when struck

components.name: Rafters / ribs
components.function: Transfer roof covering loads to walls or ground
components.position: Sloped from ridge to ground or wall plate
components.criticalNote: Snow load regions need closer spacing and stronger bend radius

components.name: Thatch, bark, or hides
components.position: Outer skin, layered shingle-style when possible
components.criticalNote: Thatch must overhang walls to reduce splash-back

components.name: Cordage lashings
components.function: Joins structural members elastically, absorbing wind shock better than rigid nails early on
components.madeFrom: Twisted bark strips, vines, or full cordage where available
components.criticalNote: Natural cordage stretches when wet — synthetic less so

components.name: Sleeping platform
components.function: Insulates from ground, improves drainage under sleeper
components.position: Rear of shelter, away from primary door draft
components.madeFrom: Poles with cross-lashed lattice and grass mattress

rawMaterials.name: Poles and stakes
earthLocations: Forests, riparian corridors, shrublands with straight stems
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Metal or composite framing when industry permits
rawMaterials.processingRequired: De-bark where insects hide; sharpen stake tips for driving

rawMaterials.name: Thatch vegetation
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Harvest dry if possible; bundle in courses; replace seasonally

rawMaterials.name: Cordage
earthLocations: Local fibre as in rope-and-cordage entry
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Pre-stretch and dry before final tensioning

buildSteps.title: Survey site and wind rose
buildSteps.description: Observe daytime breeze and local terrain funneling. Mark drainage during a rain if possible. Clear only what you must — vegetation is also camouflage and fuel.
buildSteps.warningNote: Deadfalls can shift — do not build under hung-up trees

buildSteps.title: Frame primary structure
buildSteps.description: Raise ridge on tripods or lean ridge pole against living tree (use bark spacers to avoid girdling if long-term). Seat poles in shallow depressions or on flat stones to reduce sink.
buildSteps.warningNote: Heavy lean-to loads transfer to anchors — brace against shear

buildSteps.title: Install lattice and first cover layer
buildSteps.description: Lay horizontal purlins for thatch attachment. Begin shingling from eaves upward so upper layers sheds over lower. Overlap hides like roof tiles if using skins.
buildSteps.warningNote: Gaps at eaves destroy comfort — pack first course tightly

buildSteps.title: Add vent and adjustable door
buildSteps.description: Cut high vent opposite door low slot for draft. Use hide flap or woven mat door you can partially open for smoke management.
buildSteps.warningNote: Fully sealed small shelters with open fire risk CO buildup — keep controlled leakage

buildSteps.title: Tension and storm guy
buildSteps.description: Add guy lines to windward corners anchored to buried deadmen logs. Retension after wood shrinkage.
buildSteps.warningNote: Flying stakes under tension are hazardous — wear eye protection if using modern cordage under high load

history.event: Postholes and ephemeral structures suggest deliberate windbreaks and small huts at some sites, though preservation is sparse.
Mammoth bone houses and more complex dwellings appear in cold-steppe contexts.
Seasonal roundhouses and longhouses become archaeological norms tied to sedentism.

impact:
Shelter converts climate into something humans can budget. It multiplies the effectiveness of fire and clothing, enables food storage trials, and supports infant survival — demographic growth tracks partly with housing quality.

Engineering descendants include everything from cabin carpentry to insulated panels and HVAC. The Codex treats basic shelter as the first building code: site, structure, skin, vent.
